cards.front: When implementing a complete class with operator overloading, what operators should you typically include?
cards.back: At minimum: arithmetic (+, -, *, /), compound assignment (+=, -=, *=, /=), comparison (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=), I/O (<<, >>), and possibly subscript ([]), assignment (=), and increment/decrement.

cards.text: When implementing multiple related operators, implement the {{c1::compound assignment}} version first (+=), then implement the {{c2::arithmetic}} version (+) using it to avoid code duplication.

cards.concept: Implementing a complete class with operator overloading
cards.explanation:
Steps for a well-designed class:
  • Start with constructors and destructor
  • Add comparison operators (==, <) first
  • Implement compound assignment operators (+=, -=, etc.)
  • Implement arithmetic using compound assignment
  • Add I/O operators (<<, >>)
  • Add other operators as needed ([], ++, etc.)
  • Ensure const-correctness throughout
examples: T& operator+=(const T& rhs);  // Implement first
friend T operator+(T lhs, const T& rhs) { return lhs += rhs; }  // Use +=

cards.back: Reduces code duplication and ensures consistency. operator+= contains the actual logic. operator+ creates a copy, uses +=, and returns it: T operator+(T lhs, const T& rhs) { return lhs += rhs; }
